body.text: This note is written as a source document for formalization.  All groups are written multiplicatively unless an abelian group is explicitly written additively.

body.label: Definition 1
body.definition: For a group G with identity element e, a function l : G -> R is a pseudo-length function on G iff: for every g in G, 0 <= l(g); l(e) = 0; for every g in G, l(g^{-1}) = l(g); and for every g,h in G, l(gh) <= l(g) + l(h).
body.name: PseudoLength

source.text: Let G be a group with identity element e. A function l : G -> R is a pseudo-length function on G if the following four conditions hold: nonnegativity, identity, symmetry, and triangle inequality.
source.label: Definition 1

body.text: The nonnegativity condition is included explicitly so that the codomain can be the usual real numbers in a formalization. Equivalently, one may take the codomain to be the set of nonnegative real numbers and omit the separate nonnegativity axiom.

body.label: Definition 2
body.definition: For a group G with identity element e and a pseudo-length function l : G -> R, l is a length function iff for every g in G, g != e implies 0 < l(g).
body.name: IsLength

source.text: Let G be a group with identity element e. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is a length function if, for every g in G, g != e implies 0 < l(g).
source.label: Definition 2

body.label: Definition 3
body.definition: For a group G and a pseudo-length function l : G -> R, l is homogeneous iff for every g in G and every integer n in Z, l(g^n) = |n| * l(g).
body.name: IsHomogeneousPseudoLength

source.text: Let G be a group. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is homogeneous if, for every g in G and every integer n in Z, l(g^n) = |n| l(g).
source.label: Definition 3

body.label: core-homogeneous.root.homogeneity-square
body.name: core_homogeneous_root_homogeneity_square
body.claim: ∀ (G : Type*) [Group G] (l : G → ℝ), IsHomogeneousPseudoLength G l → ∀ (g : G), l (g ^ (2 : ℤ)) = (2 : ℝ) * l g

hypothesis.assumption: l : G -> R is a homogeneous pseudo-length function.

source.text: In particular, for every g in G, homogeneity gives l(g^2)=2l(g).

body.label: Definition 8
body.definition: Define IsTorsionFree (A) for an abelian group A to mean AddCommGroup.torsion A = ⊥.
body.name: IsTorsionFree

source.text: An abelian group A is torsion-free if T(A)={0}.
source.label: Definition 8

body.label: Lemma 1

body.claim: ∀ (G : Type*) [Group G] (l : G → ℝ), IsHomogeneousPseudoLength G l → ∀ (x y : G), l (y * x * y⁻¹) = l x

proof.text:
Fix x,y in G. We first prove l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x).

Let n be a positive integer. By homogeneity, n l(yxy^{-1}) = l((yxy^{-1})^n). The group identity (yxy^{-1})^n = yx^n y^{-1} gives n l(yxy^{-1}) = l(yx^n y^{-1}). By the triangle inequality, l(yx^n y^{-1}) <= l(y) + l(x^n) + l(y^{-1}). By symmetry, l(y^{-1})=l(y). By homogeneity, l(x^n)=n l(x). Hence n l(yxy^{-1}) <= 2l(y)+n l(x). Dividing by the positive real number n gives l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x)+2l(y)/n. Since this inequality holds for every positive integer n, and 2l(y)/n tends to 0, it follows that l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x).

For the reverse inequality, define z := yxy^{-1}. Then x=y^{-1}zy. Applying the inequality already proved with z in place of x and y^{-1} in place of y gives l(x)=l(y^{-1}zy) <= l(z)=l(yxy^{-1}). The two inequalities imply l(yxy^{-1})=l(x).

source.text: Let G be a group. Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function. Then for every x,y in G, l(yxy^{-1}) = l(x).
source.label: Lemma 1

body.name: lemma_2
body.claim: ∀ (G : Type*) [Group G] (l : G → ℝ), IsHomogeneousPseudoLength G l → ∀ (w y z s t : G), let C : Nat → G := fun n => (w * y) ^ n * s⁻¹ * t * (z * w⁻¹) ^ n; ∀ n : Nat, l (C n) ≤ l s⁻¹ + l t + n * (l y + l z)

hypothesis.assumption: l : G -> R is a homogeneous pseudo-length function.
Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function

proof_steps.name: l_c_n_l_s_1_l_t
proof_steps.claim: l (C n) ≤ l s⁻¹ + l t + n * (l y + l z)

proof_steps.proof_method: Add l(y) on the left and l(z) on the right to the induction hypothesis.
proof_steps.from: l applied to y + l applied to C n + l applied to z

proof_steps.claim: l y + (l s⁻¹ + l t + n * (l y + l z)) + l z = l s⁻¹ + l t + (n + 1) * (l y + l z)
proof_steps.proof_method: side condition for l(y)+(l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)))+l(z) = l(s^{-1})+l(t)+(n+1)(l(y)+l(z))

proof_steps.claim: l y + (l s⁻¹ + l t + n * (l y + l z)) + l z = l s⁻¹ + l t + (n + 1) * (l y + l z)
proof_steps.proof_method: Arithmetic simplification and reassociation of addition.
side_conditions: The values of l lie in an ordered additive setting where addition is associative and commutative, and multiplication by natural numbers distributes over addition.

proof_steps.claim: l applied to C (n + 1) <= l applied to s^{-1} + l applied to t + (n + 1) * (l applied to y + l applied to z)
proof_steps.proof_method: By transitivity of the preceding calculation steps.
proof_steps.to: l applied to s^{-1} + l applied to t + (n + 1) * (l applied to y + l applied to z)

induction_step_proof.text: Assume the inequality holds for a natural number n. The definition gives C_{n+1}=wy C_n z w^{-1}. Equivalently, C_{n+1}=w(y C_n z)w^{-1}. By Lemma 1, applied to the element yC_nz and the conjugating element w, l(C_{n+1})=l(yC_nz). By two applications of the triangle inequality, l(yC_nz) <= l(y)+l(C_n)+l(z). By the induction hypothesis, l(C_n) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)). Combining the last two inequalities gives l(C_{n+1}) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t)+(n+1)(l(y)+l(z)). This proves the induction step.

proof.text:
We prove the claim by induction on n.

For n=0, the definition gives C_0=s^{-1}t. By the triangle inequality, l(C_0)=l(s^{-1}t) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t). This is the desired inequality for n=0.

Assume the inequality holds for a natural number n. The definition gives C_{n+1}=wy C_n z w^{-1}. Equivalently, C_{n+1}=w(y C_n z)w^{-1}. By Lemma 1, applied to the element yC_nz and the conjugating element w, l(C_{n+1})=l(yC_nz). By two applications of the triangle inequality, l(yC_nz) <= l(y)+l(C_n)+l(z). By the induction hypothesis, l(C_n) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)). Combining the last two inequalities gives l(C_{n+1}) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t)+(n+1)(l(y)+l(z)). This proves the induction step.

Therefore the inequality holds for every natural number n.

source.text: Let G be a group. Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function. Let w,y,z,s,t in G. For every natural number n, define C_n := (wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n. Then l(C_n) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)).
source.label: Lemma 2

body.label: Lemma 3
body.name: lemma_3
body.claim: ∀ (G : Type*) [Group G] (l : G → ℝ), IsHomogeneousPseudoLength G l → ∀ (a w y z s t : G), a = s * (w * y) * s⁻¹ → a = t * (z * w⁻¹) * t⁻¹ → l a ≤ (l y + l z) / 2

hypothesis.assumption: l : G -> R is a homogeneous pseudo-length function.
Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function

proof_steps.proof_method: Use the first hypothesis and the standard identity for powers of conjugates.

deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to the two conjugacy expressions for a.

proof_steps.proof_method: Use the second hypothesis and the standard identity for powers of conjugates.

deduced_from_theorem.name: standard identity for powers of conjugates

proof_steps.proof_method: Rewrite the two factors of a^n using the preceding two identities.
Apply homogeneity to a^{2n}.

deduced_from_theorem.name: homogeneity
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied with g = a and m = 2n.

proof_steps.proof_method: Use the displayed expression for a^{2n} and the triangle inequality.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For a pseudo-length function l, l(xy) <= l(x) + l(y) for all group elements x and y.
deduced_from_theorem.name: triangle inequality
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied twice to split the product into s, (wy)^n s^{-1} t (zw^{-1})^n, and t^{-1}.

proof_steps.proof_method: Apply Lemma 2.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: Let C : Nat -> G be defined by C n = (wy)^n s^{-1} t (z w^{-1})^n. For every natural number n, l(C n) <= l(s^{-1}) + l(t) + n(l(y) + l(z)).
deduced_from_theorem.name: Lemma 2
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to the fixed positive integer n.

proof_steps.proof_method: Use symmetry of l.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For a pseudo-length function l, l(g^{-1}) = l(g) for every group element g.
deduced_from_theorem.name: symmetry
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to s.

proof_steps.proof_method: Use symmetry of l.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For a pseudo-length function l, l(g^{-1}) = l(g) for every group element g.
deduced_from_theorem.name: symmetry
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to t.

proof_steps.proof_method: Combine the previous inequalities and rewrite inverse lengths by symmetry.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: If x = y, y <= z, z <= u, and equalities allow rewriting u, then x <= the rewritten upper bound.
deduced_from_theorem.name: transitivity and substitution for inequalities
deduced_from_theorem.description: Combines the equality from homogeneity, the triangle inequality bound, Lemma 2, and symmetry rewrites.

proof_steps.proof_method: Divide the inequality by the positive scalar 2n and simplify algebraically.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: If n > 0 and 2n x <= 2A + nB, then x <= B/2 + A/n.
deduced_from_theorem.name: division by positive scalar and algebraic rearrangement
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied with x = l(a), A = l(s)+l(t), and B = l(y)+l(z).

proof_steps.proof_method: Let n tend to infinity in the preceding inequality.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: If x <= B + A/n for every positive integer n and A/n tends to 0, then x <= B.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Archimedean limiting argument
deduced_from_theorem.description: Uses that (l(s)+l(t))/n tends to 0 as n tends to infinity.

proof.text: Let n be a positive integer. From the two hypotheses and the standard identity for powers of conjugates, we have a^n=s(wy)^n s^{-1} and a^n=t(zw^{-1})^n t^{-1}. Hence a^{2n}=a^n a^n =s(wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n t^{-1}. By homogeneity, 2n l(a)=l(a^{2n}). Using the displayed expression for a^{2n} and the triangle inequality, l(a^{2n}) <= l(s)+l((wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n)+l(t^{-1}). By Lemma 2, l((wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)). By symmetry, l(s^{-1})=l(s) and l(t^{-1})=l(t). Therefore 2n l(a) <= 2l(s)+2l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)). Dividing by 2n gives l(a) <= (l(y)+l(z))/2+(l(s)+l(t))/n. Since this inequality holds for every positive integer n, and (l(s)+l(t))/n tends to 0, we obtain l(a) <= (l(y)+l(z))/2.

source.text: Let G be a group. Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function. Let a,w,y,z,s,t in G. Assume a=s(wy)s^{-1} and a=t(zw^{-1})t^{-1}. Then l(a) <= (l(y)+l(z))/2.
source.label: Lemma 3

body.text: The next lemma is the combinatorial input used to prove that homogeneous pseudo-length functions vanish on commutators.  It is stated as a separate lemma because its proof is a finite calculation in the group laws followed by Lemma 3.

body.label: Lemma 4
body.name: lemma_4
body.claim: ∀ (G : Type*) [Group G] (l : G → ℝ), IsHomogeneousPseudoLength G l → ∀ (x y : G), let c : G := x * y * x⁻¹ * y⁻¹; let f : ℤ → ℤ → ℝ := fun m k => l (x^m * c^k); ∀ (m k : ℤ), f m k ≤ (f (m - 1) k + f (m + 1) (k - 1)) / 2

hypothesis.assumption: l : G -> R is a homogeneous pseudo-length function.
Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function
Fix x,y in G and set c:=[x,y]

proof_steps.assumption: m and k are arbitrary integers

proof_steps.statement: Define a to be x^m * c^k.
Define u to be x^(m - 1) * c^k.
Define v to be inverse y * x^m * c^(k - 1) * x * y.

proof_steps.claim: a is conjugate to w * u
proof_steps.proof_method: Since a = w * u, equality implies conjugacy.

proof_steps.proof_method: Substitute the definitions of v and w.

proof_steps.proof_method: Named local obligation from unresolved claim dependency.
Rewrite the commutator identity c = x y x^{-1} y^{-1} by multiplying on the right by y.

proof_steps.name: a_x_m_c_k

Use xyx^{-1} = c y inside the expression for v * w^{-1}, combine c^(k-1) c = c^k, and substitute a = x^m c^k.

Rearrange by conjugating with y.

proof_steps.proof_method: Use the displayed conjugation equation.
Apply Lemma 3 with s = e, t = y, y = u, and z = v.

proof_steps.proof_method: By the definition of u and the definition of f.

source.text: v = y^{-1} x^m c^(k-1) x y

proof_steps.proof_method: Substitute the definition of v and cancel y y^{-1} on both sides in the product y * (y^{-1} x^m c^(k-1) x y) * y^{-1}.
Use associativity, cancellation of x x^{-1}, and the exponent law x * x^m = x^(m+1), preserving the given order of factors.

proof_steps.claim: v is conjugate to x^(m+1) c^(k-1)
proof_steps.proof_method: The first equation shows v is conjugate to x^m c^(k-1) x, and the second shows x^m c^(k-1) x is conjugate to x^(m+1)c^(k-1); combine these conjugacies.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For all group elements h b g, h = g * b * g^{-1} implies h is conjugate to b; and for all group elements p q r, if p is conjugate to q and q is conjugate to r, then p is conjugate to r.
deduced_from_theorem.description: Combines the two displayed conjugations to show v is conjugate to x^(m+1)c^(k-1).

proof_steps.claim: l applied to v = f applied to m + 1 and k - 1
proof_steps.proof_method: Apply Lemma 1 to the conjugacy between v and x^(m+1)c^(k-1), then use the definition of f.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For every x,y in G, l(y * x * y^{-1}) = l(x).
deduced_from_theorem.name: Lemma 1
deduced_from_theorem.description: Conjugate elements have the same length.

proof_steps.name: a_x_m_c_k
proof_steps.proof_method: Named local obligation from unresolved claim dependency.

proof_steps.claim: f applied to m and k <= (f applied to m - 1 and k + f applied to m + 1 and k - 1) / 2
proof_steps.proof_method: Substitute l(a)=f(m,k), l(u)=f(m-1,k), and l(v)=f(m+1,k-1) into the inequality from Lemma 3.

proof.text:
Fix m,k in Z. Define a:=x^m c^k, w:=x, u:=x^{m-1}c^k, and v:=y^{-1}x^m c^{k-1}xy. Then wu=x(x^{m-1}c^k)=x^m c^k=a. Thus a is conjugate to wu, because a=wu.

We next prove that a is conjugate to vw^{-1}. Since w=x, vw^{-1}=y^{-1}x^m c^{k-1}xyx^{-1}. Using the commutator identity c=xyx^{-1}y^{-1}, we have xyx^{-1}=cy. Therefore vw^{-1}=y^{-1}x^m c^{k-1}cy=y^{-1}x^m c^k y=y^{-1}ay. Hence a=y(vw^{-1})y^{-1}, so a is conjugate to vw^{-1}.

Lemma 3, applied with s=e, t=y, y=u, and z=v, gives l(a)<= (l(u)+l(v))/2. By the definition of u, l(u)=l(x^{m-1}c^k)=f(m-1,k). It remains to identify l(v). The element v is conjugate to x^{m+1}c^{k-1} because yvy^{-1}=x^m c^{k-1}x and x(x^m c^{k-1}x)x^{-1}=x^{m+1}c^{k-1}. By Lemma 1, conjugate elements have the same length. Therefore l(v)=l(x^{m+1}c^{k-1})=f(m+1,k-1). Substituting the formulas for l(u) and l(v) into the inequality from Lemma 3 gives f(m,k)<= (f(m-1,k)+f(m+1,k-1))/2.

source.text: Let G be a group. Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function. Fix x,y in G and set c:=[x,y]. Define f : Z x Z -> R by f(m,k):=l(x^m c^k). Then for every m,k in Z, f(m,k) <= (f(m-1,k)+f(m+1,k-1))/2.
source.label: Lemma 4
